---
name: review-naming-conventions
description: Review naming conventions in a PR diff, file, or module against the team's established patterns. Use when asked to "check naming", "review names", "are these names consistent", or "naming convention review".
di_signal: DESIGN
maturity_required: early
status: approved
---

# Review Naming Conventions

## Overview

Reviews naming consistency in a PR diff, file, or module — not against generic
style guides, but against the conventions the team has already established in
the codebase. The output is a structured DESIGN signal assessment that surfaces
violations, inconsistencies, and missed intent in names.

The discipline here is reading before judging. Naming conventions are assessed
against what the team is already doing — not what a style guide says they should
do. Consistency with the existing codebase always takes precedence over textbook
correctness.

## When to Use

- When a PR introduces new identifiers that may not match the team's conventions
- When reviewing code generated by AI tools (which apply generic naming, not team conventions)
- When a module has grown inconsistently and naming has drifted across files
- When a new developer's first PR needs a naming check before merge
- Any time the user says: "check naming", "are these names consistent", "naming
  convention review", "do these names match our style", "review identifiers"

## Instructions

### Step 1: Establish the Team's Conventions

**Read first — always:**
1. Load `.dev-iq/config.yaml` → `stack.language` and `stack.framework`
2. Read 2–3 existing files in the same layer or module as the target code
3. Identify the conventions actually in use — not the defaults from this instruction file

**Record what you observe:**

| Dimension | Convention observed in codebase |
|-----------|--------------------------------|
| Classes / types | [PascalCase / snake_case / observed pattern] |
| Functions / methods | [camelCase / snake_case / observed pattern] |
| Constants | [UPPER_SNAKE_CASE / observed pattern] |
| Booleans | [is_ / has_ / can_ prefix observed?] |
| Private members | [_ prefix / __ / access modifier only] |
| Collections | [plural observed?] |
| Event handlers | [on_ / handle_ prefix observed?] |
| Interfaces / abstractions | [I prefix / -able suffix / observed pattern] |

If the codebase is new with fewer than 5 files, apply language defaults from
`di-code-standards.instructions.md` and flag that conventions are still forming.

### Step 2: Scan the Target

Accept one of:
- **PR diff** — scan all new and modified identifiers in the diff
- **File or module path** — scan all public identifiers in the specified file(s)
- **Paste** — scan identifiers in pasted code

**Identifiers to check:**
- Class and type names
- Function and method names
- Variable and parameter names (especially booleans)
- Constants and enums
- File and directory names (if visible in the diff)
- Interface and abstract type names

### Step 3: Classify Each Finding

| Severity | Definition |
|----------|-----------|
| 🔴 High | Wrong case for the identifier type (e.g., class in snake_case, constant in camelCase); directly contradicts the team's established pattern |
| 🟡 Medium | Inconsistent with established patterns but not a case error (e.g., boolean without `is_`/`has_` prefix when all others use it; collection named singular) |
| ⚪ Low | Clarity opportunity — the name is technically correct but ambiguous or underspecified (e.g., `data`, `result`, `temp`, `flag`) |

**Do not flag:**
- Names that match the team's actual convention even if they differ from this file's defaults
- Abbreviations that are well-established in the domain (e.g., `url`, `id`, `dto`)
- Names in external library interfaces or generated code

### Step 4: Produce the Report

Group findings by file. For each finding: location, current name, severity, and
a specific suggested name — not a generic rule citation.

At **Early maturity**: include a coaching note for each High finding explaining
why the naming inconsistency matters (confusion for future readers, broken
search patterns, misidentified type on first read).

At **Mid/Higher maturity**: structured findings only.

## Inputs Required

| Input | Source | Required |
|-------|--------|----------|
| PR diff, file path, or code paste | User provides | Yes |
| Stack language | `.dev-iq/config.yaml` | Auto-read |
| Adjacent codebase files for convention baseline | Auto-read from context | Required |
| Work item ID | User provides | Recommended |
| Maturity tier | `.dev-iq/config.yaml` | Auto-read |

## Output Format

```
## Naming Convention Review — [PR title / file / module]
Work Item: [AB#XXXX | PROJ-XXX | #456 | "none provided"]
Language: [TypeScript | Python | Go | ...]
Convention baseline: [read from [N] existing files in [layer/module]]
Assessed: [date]

---

### Convention Baseline (observed in codebase)

| Dimension | Pattern |
|-----------|---------|
| Classes / types | PascalCase |
| Functions / methods | camelCase |
| Constants | UPPER_SNAKE_CASE |
| Booleans | is_ / has_ prefix |
| Private members | _ prefix |
| Collections | plural nouns |

---

### Findings

#### [file path]

| Line | Current name | Suggested name | Severity | Reason |
|------|-------------|----------------|----------|--------|
| [L42] | [currentName] | [suggestedName] | 🔴 High | [specific reason — not a generic rule] |
| [L67] | [anotherName] | [betterName] | 🟡 Medium | [specific reason] |
| [L89] | [vagueResult] | [descriptiveName] | ⚪ Low | [specific reason] |

[Repeat per file]

---

### Summary

| Severity | Count |
|----------|-------|
| 🔴 High | [N] |
| 🟡 Medium | [N] |
| ⚪ Low | [N] |

**DESIGN Signal: [STRONG | WEAK | UNGRADED]**

STRONG: no High findings, Medium findings are isolated
WEAK: one or more High findings, or systemic Medium findings
UNGRADED: convention baseline could not be established (new codebase, <5 files)

@di-review-required
```

## Examples

### Example 1: TypeScript PR with Naming Inconsistencies

**User says:** `/review-naming-conventions` — PR diff for `feature/payment-retry`

**Convention baseline:** Read from 4 existing service files — all use camelCase
methods, PascalCase classes, `is_`/`has_` for booleans, `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE` constants.

**Findings excerpt:**

```
## Naming Convention Review — feature/payment-retry
Language: TypeScript
Convention baseline: read from 4 existing files in src/payments/

---

### Findings

#### src/payments/RetryHandler.ts

| Line | Current name | Suggested name | Severity | Reason |
|------|-------------|----------------|----------|--------|
| L12 | MAX_RETRY_count | MAX_RETRY_COUNT | 🔴 High | Constants use UPPER_SNAKE_CASE in this codebase; mixed case breaks the pattern |
| L34 | retry_payment | retryPayment | 🔴 High | Methods use camelCase; snake_case is inconsistent with all 4 baseline files |
| L51 | paymentFailed | isPaymentFailed | 🟡 Medium | All boolean variables use is_ prefix in existing code; this one doesn't |
| L78 | data | paymentAttempt | ⚪ Low | `data` is ambiguous at this scope; the specific type is clear from context |

---

### Summary

| Severity | Count |
|----------|-------|
| 🔴 High | 2 |
| 🟡 Medium | 1 |
| ⚪ Low | 1 |

**DESIGN Signal: WEAK** — 2 High findings must be resolved before merge.

@di-review-required
```

---

### Example 2: Python Module — Convention Baseline Established

**Baseline observation:** snake_case functions, PascalCase classes, `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE`
constants, `is_`/`has_` booleans, private members with `__` prefix (dunder pattern).

**Finding:**

```
| L23 | processOrder | process_order | 🔴 High | Functions use snake_case in this codebase; camelCase is AI-generated default, not team convention |
| L41 | __orderValid | __is_order_valid | 🟡 Medium | Private booleans use is_ prefix with __ prefix in existing code |

DESIGN Signal: WEAK
```

---

### Example 3: New Codebase — Convention Forming

```
Convention baseline: UNGRADED — fewer than 5 existing files; applying language
defaults for TypeScript (camelCase methods, PascalCase classes, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE constants).

Note (Early maturity coaching): With a new codebase, now is the time to establish
conventions deliberately. The patterns in this PR will become the baseline future
developers match. Consider a short team agreement before more files are added.
```

---

## Common Rationalizations

These are the statements that get naming review skipped. Rebut them.

| Rationalization | Reality |
|----------------|---------|
| "Naming is subjective — any consistent style is fine" | Consistency is the goal, not the starting state. A PR that introduces a second naming style into a codebase with one makes the next developer's job harder, not just different. |
| "The linter handles naming" | Linters catch case violations in languages with strict conventions. They don't catch names that are technically correct but inconsistent with the team's established patterns, ambiguous, or misidentified by type. |
| "AI-generated code follows standard conventions" | AI-generated code follows the language's generic conventions — not the team's specific patterns. A generated Python file will use snake_case, but it won't know that this team prefixes all service classes with `Domain`. |
| "Renaming is just cosmetic — it can wait" | Names are the primary interface between code and its readers. A method named `process` in a codebase where everything else is `processPayment`, `processOrder`, `processRefund` is a navigation and comprehension failure waiting to happen. |

## Governance

- Convention baseline must be read from the actual codebase before any finding
  is issued — generic rule citations without reading the team's code is always
  a DESIGN UNGRADED assessment
- High findings always produce DESIGN WEAK — they must be resolved before merge
- Low findings are optional improvements — never block the verdict on Low alone
- Never flag names in external library interfaces, generated code, or third-party
  contracts — those are not the team's naming decisions
- At Early maturity, every High finding includes a coaching note explaining the
  reader impact of the inconsistency
- UNGRADED is appropriate when a convention baseline cannot be established —
  do not apply generic defaults and call it STRONG

## Related Skills

- `/review-code` — full four-layer code review; naming is assessed as part of
  the DESIGN signal in that review
- `/refactor-code` — when naming findings are systemic across a module, a
  targeted refactoring plan may be needed to resolve them cohesively
- `/onboard-codebase` — the onboarding guide documents established naming
  conventions as part of the codebase orientation
- `/dev-iq-tailor` — client config can add naming rules specific to the team's
  conventions (`naming.boolean_prefix`, `naming.service_suffix`, etc.)
